coredata.dc:title: Increase and enrichment of active electrons by carbon dots induced to improve TiO2 photocatalytic hydrogen production activity
coredata.dc:creator: Huang, Xiaolin
Sun, Lianhang
Liu, Xing
Ge, Min
Zhao, Bin
Bai, Yibing
Wang, Yuning
Han, Shiyan
Li, Yudong
Han, Youqi
Zhang, Chunlei
coredata.dc:description: Recombination of photogenerated carriers severely reduces the efficiency of photocatalytic reactions. Herein, Bio-CDs/TiO2 were produced using the hydrothermal method to tightly load biomass-based carbon dots (Bio-CDs) onto the surface of TiO2 via Ti-O-C bonds. Finite-difference time-domain (FDTD) simulations showed that the Bio-CDs act as electron storage pools in the Bio-CDs/TiO2, enhancing optical absorption and promoting the transfer and storage of photogenerated electrons from TiO2 to the Bio-CDs, and thus effectively inhibiting the recombination of electron-hole pairs. Photocatalytic hydrogen production by the Bio-CDs/TiO2 was significantly improved compared with that by pristine TiO2 (603.92 μmol h−1 g−1 vs 94.18 μmol h−1 g−1) and, importantly, the Bio-CDs/TiO2 performed well over several cycles. Surprisingly, Bio-CDs/TiO2 also exhibits nice catalytic performance for hydrogen production under the combined action of photoelectricity, and its Tafel slope was significantly lower than that of pristine TiO2, which confirmed that the same research potential of Bio-CDs/TiO2 in the field of photoelectrocatalysis. This work introduces new ways to study the synergistic effects of multiple mechanisms (enhanced optical absorption, increased separation of photogenerated carriers and promotion of transfer and storage of photogenerated electrons) in the enhancement of photocatalytic and photoelectrocatalytic activity of semiconductors by carbon dots.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Biomass-based carbon dots
TiO2 composite
Light absorption enhancement
Electrons harvest
H2 generation

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (32171715), the Natural Science Foundation of Heilongjiang Province (LH2022C012) and the Heilongjiang Postdoctoral Foundation (LBH-Q21003). The authors are grateful for the funding.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XRD spectra of TiO2, TB5, TB7, TB9, TB12 and Bio-CDs.
SEM images of pristine TiO2 (a) and TB9 (b); TEM (c) and HR-TEM (d) images of TB9.

Full survey spectrum of TB9 (a); C 1 s spectrum of TB9 (b); N 1 s spectrum of TB9 (c); O 1 s spectrum of TB9 (d); Ti 2p spectra of pristine TiO2 and TB9 (e); FT-IR spectra of pristine TiO2, TB5, TB7, TB9 and TB12 (f).

Photocatalytic hydrogen production. Photocatalytic activity of different samples (a); Histograms of different samples (b); Photocatalytic hydrogen production by TB9 over four cycles (c); Histogram of TB9 (d).
XRD spectra (a); FT-IR spectra (b) and Ti 2p spectra (c) of TB9 before and after photocatalytic reaction.
Calculated band gaps of pristine TiO2 and TB9 photoanodes based on Kubelka-Munk function (a); High-resolution XPS valence band spectra of pristine TiO2 and TB9 (b).

Transient photocurrent responses (a) and EIS Nyquist plots (b) of the different samples; LSV curves of the different samples (c); (d) The corresponding Tafel slope transformed from (c); PL spectra of the different samples (e); Time-resolved PL spectra of TiO2 and TB9 (f).

Electric field distributions calculated using the FDTD method at the interfaces of TiO2 (a) and Bio-CDs/TiO2 (b). The direction of the electric field was polarized along the X-axis and then incident on the Z-axis. Mechanism of enhancement of photocatalytic hydrogen production by Bio-CDs-TiO2 (c).
Preparation of Bio-CDs/TiO2 and photocatalytic hydrogen production.

head.ce:title: Increase and enrichment of active electrons by carbon dots induced to improve TiO2 photocatalytic hydrogen production activity

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Bio-Based Material Science & Technology, Northeast Forestry University, Hexing Road 26, Harbin 150040, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Bio-Based Material Science & Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Bio-Based Material Science & Technology, Northeast Forestry University, Hexing Road 26, Harbin 150040, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: MIIT Key Laboratory of Critical Materials Technology for New Energy Conversion and Storage, School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Harbin Institute of Technology, Harbin 150001, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: MIIT Key Laboratory of Critical Materials Technology for New Energy Conversion and Storage
School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: MIIT Key Laboratory of Critical Materials Technology for New Energy Conversion and Storage, School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Harbin Institute of Technology, Harbin 150001, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Increase and enrichment of active electrons by carbon dots through multi-mechanism synergy (enhanced optical absorption, increased separation of photogenerated carriers and promotion of transfer and storage of photogenerated electrons) improves photocatalytic and photoelectrocatalytic hydrogen generation by composite Bio-CDs/TiO2 catalyst.

Recombination of photogenerated carriers severely reduces the efficiency of photocatalytic reactions. Herein, Bio-CDs/TiO2 were produced using the hydrothermal method to tightly load biomass-based carbon dots (Bio-CDs) onto the surface of TiO2 via Ti-O-C bonds. Finite-difference time-domain (FDTD) simulations showed that the Bio-CDs act as electron storage pools in the Bio-CDs/TiO2, enhancing optical absorption and promoting the transfer and storage of photogenerated electrons from TiO2 to the Bio-CDs, and thus effectively inhibiting the recombination of electron-hole pairs. Photocatalytic hydrogen production by the Bio-CDs/TiO2 was significantly improved compared with that by pristine TiO2 (603.92 μmol h−1 g−1 vs 94.18 μmol h−1 g−1) and, importantly, the Bio-CDs/TiO2 performed well over several cycles. Surprisingly, Bio-CDs/TiO2 also exhibits nice catalytic performance for hydrogen production under the combined action of photoelectricity, and its Tafel slope was significantly lower than that of pristine TiO2, which confirmed that the same research potential of Bio-CDs/TiO2 in the field of photoelectrocatalysis. This work introduces new ways to study the synergistic effects of multiple mechanisms (enhanced optical absorption, increased separation of photogenerated carriers and promotion of transfer and storage of photogenerated electrons) in the enhancement of photocatalytic and photoelectrocatalytic activity of semiconductors by carbon dots.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Hydrogen fuel is one of the most promising candidates for renewable green energy since it produces zero carbon emission and has both high calorific value and high energy density [1,2]. The current focus in hydrogen production is photocatalytic splitting of water, a process that is clean, cheap and environmentally friendly [3–6]. Numerous excellent photocatalysts have been described, with TiO2 widely regarded as the “gold standard” because of its excellent photocatalytic performance, good chemical stability, lack of toxicity and low cost [7–9]. However, because of the rapid recombination of photogenerated carriers and the narrow light response range, the improvement in efficiency of solar energy conversion by TiO2 is limited [7,8]. This problem can be overcome by the use of cocatalysts, which provide efficient active sites that catalyze the reaction by lowering the activation energy and trapping charge carriers, thereby inhibiting the recombination of photogenerated electron-hole pairs [10]. Despite improvements in performance, the commonly used cocatalysts are still not ideal since they are mainly metals or metallic compounds, which are expensive and toxic [5,11,12]. There is, therefore a real need for sustainable, lower-cost and multi-functional cocatalysts to improve photocatalytic performance.

Carbon dots are zero-dimensional carbon-based nanomaterials with superior optical properties [13–16] that have recently become the focus of much attention [17–21]. Notably, carbon dots have been shown to be effective in promoting photocatalytic hydrogen evolution by semiconductor materials under visible light irradiation [8]. When carbon dots and semiconductors are combined for photocatalysis, strong chemical interactions are formed between the carbon dots and the semiconductors, which improve the stability of the composites and increase the efficiency of charge transfer, resulting in higher photocatalytic activity. The mechanisms by which carbon dots enhance the photocatalytic activity of semiconductors are, however, not fully understood. In most cases, the carbon dots are described as having a single function, such as enhancing optical absorption, increasing the separation of photogenerated carriers or promoting the transfer and storage of photogenerated electrons [22–24]. Studies on the mechanisms of synergy between the multiple roles of carbon dots in photocatalytic systems are lacking and there is a real need for further research in this area.

All materials and reagents were used as received, without further purification. Dehydroabietic acid (99.06%) was purchased from Jusheng Technology Co., Ltd. (Hubei, China). Ethanolamine were purchased from Kemiou Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Tianjin, China). Pristine TiO2 was purchased from Aladdin Chemistry Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Nafion’D-520 dispersion was purchased from Alfa Aesar (China) Chemical Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Na2SO4, CH3OH and CH3CH2OH were purchased from Kemiou Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Tianjin, China). Deionized water was produced using a Clever-Q30 UT ultrapure water system (Zhiang Instrument Co., Ltd., Shanghai, China).

The Bio-CDs were prepared as previously described [25]. Briefly, dehydroabietic acid (0.4 g), ethanolamine (1.0 mL) and deionized water (70 mL) were placed in a 100 mL Teflon-lined autoclave and heated to 200 °C for 300 min. The reaction mixture was then allowed to cool naturally to room temperature and the resulting suspension was freeze-dried to provide the Bio-CDs.

TiO2 (0.4 g), deionized water (20 mL) and absolute ethanol (6 mL) were placed in a 50 mL Teflon-lined autoclave. A solution of Bio-CDs in alcohol (1 mg mL−1) was then added to the autoclave and the mixture was stirred with a glass rod. The mixture was heated to 140 °C for 4 h and then allowed to cool naturally to room temperature. The contents of the autoclave were poured into a disposable beaker and dried in a vacuum drying oven at 40 °C to provide the Bio-CDs/TiO2 composites. A series of Bio-CDs/TiO2 nanocomposites were prepared using different volumes of the solution of Bio-CDs in alcohol (V Bio-CDs = 5, 7, 9 and 12 mL), which were designated TB5, TB7, TB9 and TB12, respectively.

Powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of the samples were obtained using an X’Pert3 powder X-ray diffractometer (Malvern Panalytical Ltd., Malvern, UK). The morphologies of the photocatalysts were investigated using a JEM-2100 transmission electron microscope (JEOL Ltd., Tokyo, Japan) and an Apreo S scanning electron microscope (Thermo Fisher Scientific Inc., Waltham, MA, USA). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was carried out using an ESCALAB 250Xi spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific, New York, NY, USA). Fourier transform-infrared (FT-IR) spectra were recorded using a Fourier FT-IR spectrometer (PerkinElmer Inc., Waltham, MA, USA). UV–Vis diffuse reflectance spectra of the samples were recorded using a TU-1950 UV–Vis spectrofluorometer equipped with an integrating sphere attachment (Persee General Instrument Co., Ltd., Beijing, China). Photoluminescence spectra were recorded using an LS55 fluorescence spectrometer (PerkinElmer Inc., Waltham, MA, USA) and fluorescence decay curves were recorded using a QM8000 fluorescence spectrometer (Horiba Jobin Yvon Inc., Edison, NJ, USA).

Photoelectrochemical measurements were performed using a CHI 760E electrochemical analyzer in a standard three-electrode configuration, with a Pt sheet as the counter electrode, a Ag/AgCl electrode as the reference electrode and a working electrode. The working electrodes were prepared by dispersing the sample (4 mg) in a mixture of ethanol (400 μL) and deionized water (400 μL) and immediately adding Nafion’D-520 dispersion (5 μL). The mixture was sonicated until evenly mixed and then coated onto a 2 cm × 1 cm piece of fluorine-doped tin oxide glass and dried at 60 °C in a vacuum drying oven. Aqueous Na2SO4 aqueous solution (0.5 M) was used as the electrolyte and the irradiation source was a 300 W Xenon lamp (AM1.5, 100 mW cm−2) with a cut-off filter (λ > 380 nm).

Photocatalytic hydrogen production was conducted in a self-assembled reaction system. In a typical photocatalytic reaction, Bio-CDs/TiO2 composite (50 mg) was added to water (100 mL) containing 15 vol% methanol as the sacrificial reagent. The 300 W Xenon lamp was placed vertically on the top of the reactor and the temperature of the reactant solution was maintained at 25 °C by a recirculated cooling system. The amount of hydrogen produced was measured using a gas chromatograph every hour over a 5-hour cycle. After 5 h, the hydrogen was discharged and the reactor was refilled with argon. The whole hydrogen production experiment was carried out for a total of four 5-hour cycles.

The XRD spectra of pristine TiO2, Bio-CDs and Bio-CDs/TiO2 composites (TB5, TB7, TB9 and TB12) are shown in Fig. 1 . The diffraction peaks at 25.33°, 37.84°, 48.03°, 53.98°, 55.14°, 62.72° and 68.92° correspond to the (101), (004), (200), (105), (211), (204) and (116) planes of anatase TiO2, respectively [26–28] and the diffraction peaks at 27.37° and 36.08° correspond to the (110) and (101) planes of rutile TiO2, respectively [27–29]. The positions of the diffraction peaks of pristine TiO2 and Bio-CDs/TiO2 composites (TB5, TB7, TB9 and TB12) are consistent. Because the composite samples contain only small amounts of Bio-CDs, diffraction peaks due to the Bio-CDs were not observed in the composite samples since they were masked by the TiO2 diffraction peaks.

Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images showed that the microscopic morphology of TB9 was the same as that of pristine TiO2, with both materials being composed of spherical particles (Fig. 2
a, 2b), demonstrating that doping with Bio-CDs does not affect the morphology of the TiO2. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) showed that the Bio-CDs nanoparticles were deposited on the surface of the TiO2 (Fig. 2 c), which is considered to be important for photocatalysis [30]. The lattice spacings of 0.21 nm, 0.33 nm and 0.35 nm in the corresponding high resolution (HR) TEM image (Fig. 2 d) corresponded well with the lattice spacings of the (100) plane of graphite carbon [25], the (110) plane of rutile TiO2 [28,31] and the (101) plane of anatase TiO2 [8,27,28,32], respectively, further confirming that the Bio-CDs and TiO2 have been successfully compounded. The Bio-CDs can, therefore, achieve the function of transferring and storing photogenerated electrons by virtue of some of their own characteristics.

The composition and chemical state of pristine TiO2 and TB9 were analyzed by XPS. Pristine TiO2 contains the elements of Ti, O and C ( Fig. S1 ), with the small amount of C originating from pollution of the spectrometer with C. The XPS spectrum of TB9 showed strong signals for C 1 s, O 1 s and Ti 2p, together with a weak signal for N 1 s (Fig. 3
a). The C content of TB9 was significantly higher than that of pristine TiO2 and, additionally, a peak for the element N appeared, further confirming the successful combination of Bio-CDs and TiO2. The high-resolution C 1 s spectrum of TB9 showed three peaks at 284.6 eV, 286 eV and 288.5 eV (Fig. 3 b), attributable to C–C/C = C, C-O/C-N and C = O bonds, respectively [8,21,25,33,34]. The wide and asymmetric high-resolution N 1 s spectrum of TB9 (Fig. 3 c) could be deconvoluted into two dominant peaks located at 398.8 eV and 400.8 eV, which originate from the Bio-CDs and can be attributed to pyridine N and pyrrole N, respectively [25,35]. The presence of the element N has been shown to improve photocatalytic performance [30]. The O 1 s spectrum (Fig. 3 d) can be fitted to three peaks at 529.1 eV, 531.5 eV and 533.3 eV, which can be attributed to Ti-O bonds on the structure, superposition of C = O bonds in the Bio-CDs and –OH bonds adsorbed on the surface, respectively [8,25]. The Ti 2p spectrum of TB9 showed peaks at 457.9 eV and 463.7 eV, belonging to Ti 2p3/2 and Ti 2p1/2, which have higher binding energies than the Ti 2p3/2 (457.6 eV) and Ti 2p1/2 (463.4 eV) peaks of pristine TiO2 (Fig. 3 e). This confirms that the coupling between Bio-CDs and TiO2 is achieved through Ti-O-C bonds [8], which has been reported to improve photocatalytic hydrogen production [8]. Relying on rich bonding characteristics, inside the TB, the active excitons could transfer to the interfaces rather than the recombination, thus to improve the performances of catalysis.

The FT-IR spectra of pristine TiO2 and the different composite samples (TB5, TB7, TB9 and TB12) are shown in Fig. 3 f. The main peak at 600–700 cm−1 corresponds to Ti-O stretching vibrations and the Ti-O-Ti bridge stretching mode [23], and the weak absorption peak at 1637 cm−1 is due to hydroxyl groups and physical absorption of water on the surface [23,26]. As the proportion of Bio-CDs was increased, the FT-IR spectra of the samples showed peaks at 3200–3400 cm−1, 2900–2990 cm−1 and 1393 cm−1, which are attributable to stretching vibrations of –NH-/–OH bonds [25,35,36], stretching vibrations of –CH3/–CH2 groups [20,36] and vibrations of C-N bonds [25,35], respectively. The absorption peak at 1637 cm−1 is increased by the presence of C = O bonds [20,25,35]. The appearance and enhancement of these peaks are all caused by the loading of Bio-CDs, which improves photocatalytic hydrogen production. All of the above results confirm the successful preparation of Bio-CDs/TiO2 composites. Furthermore, staggered bonding could decrease the hinder of excitons transfer, accelerating the catalysis reactions.

Photocatalytic hydrogen production by pristine TiO2, TB5, TB7, TB9 and TB12 is shown in Fig. 4
a (the Bio-CDs themselves were found not to catalyze hydrogen production and so are not shown in Fig. 4 a). Hydrogen production by Bio-CDs/TiO2 composites gradually increased as the proportion of Bio-CDs in the photocatalyst increased, reaching a maximum with TB9 and then decreasing (Fig. 4 a, 4b). From Fig. 4 a, it can be seen that with the extension of time, the hydrogen production rate of Bio-CDs/TiO2 per hour gradually increases, which may be attributed to the increase in absorbed photon flux [37], promoting the separation of photo-generated carriers, accelerating the electron transfer rate, thus improving the hydrogen production rate. Hydrogen production using TB9 as photocatalyst (603.92 μmol h−1 g−1) was 6.41-fold higher than the relatively poor inherent hydrogen production by pristine TiO2 (94.18 μmol h−1 g−1). The times of the increase in photocatalytic hydrogen production is higher than previously reported values for other photocatalysts ( Table S1 ), indicating that loading of Bio-CDs is largely responsible for enhancing the catalytic production of hydrogen by the composite photocatalyst under visible light, and providing strong evidence that Bio-CDs can effectively improve the efficiency of photocatalytic hydrogen production.

Having established the excellent performance of TB9 for catalytic hydrogen production under visible light, we next investigated the cycling stability of TB9. As shown in Fig. 4 c, TB9 showed good hydrogen production stability over 20 h (4 consecutive cycles), with an average value of 595.88 μmol h−1 g−1 (Fig. 4 d) that is gratifying close to the initial value (603.92 μmol h−1 g−1). After photocatalysis, the XRD peaks (Fig. 5
a) and characteristic absorption peaks in the FT-IR spectrum (Fig. 5 b) of TB9 were essentially the same as those of the initial sample. The high resolution XPS spectrum of Ti 2p also maintained a very similar binding energy to the initial sample (Fig. 5 c). These data demonstrate that as-prepared TB9 has remarkable stability during photocatalytic hydrogen production and all of the above results indicate that doping of TiO2 with Bio-CDs effectively improves photocatalytic hydrogen production and achieves good cycling stability. It is, therefore, important to explore the mechanism(s) by which TB9 enhances photocatalytic hydrogen production.

The UV–Vis diffuse reflectance and absorption spectra of TB9 and TiO2 are shown in Fig. S2 . The UV–Vis absorption edge of pristine TiO2 is about 400 nm, which is due to the intrinsic band gap [23]. By loading Bio-CDs, the light absorption threshold of Bio-CDs/TiO2 composite expands to 420 nm ( Fig. S2
inset), which is in the visible light range. Increased absorption of light would be regarded as one of the reasons for the enhancement in photocatalytic hydrogen production and also indicates that the Bio-CDs play a role in enhancing photocatalytic hydrogen production by TiO2 [38,39]. Significantly, according to the Kubelka-Munk function, the band gap of TB9 is smaller than that of pristine TiO2 (3.15 eV vs 3.21 eV, Fig. 6
a). Calculations based on the high-resolution XPS valence band spectra show that the valence band values of pristine TiO2 and TB9 are 2.53 eV and 1.79 eV, respectively (Fig. 6 b). Using formula (1), the conduction band value of TB9 (-1.36 eV) is calculated to be more negative than that of pristine TiO2 (-0.68 eV), which means that TB9 is more favorable for the hydrogen evolution reaction [24].
(1)
E g
=
E
V B
-
E
C B
where Eg is the gap width, ECB is the conduction band value and EVB is the valence band value.

The loading of Bio-CDs promotes the separation and transfer of TiO2 photo-generated charge carriers, which are important contributors to photocatalytic performance. To further investigate the charge separation and transfer efficiency of the samples, we carried out photoelectrochemical analyses, including transient photocurrent responses, electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS), linear-sweep voltammetry (LSV), photoluminescence (PL) measurements and time-resolved PL measurements. The transient photocurrent of the Bio-CDs/TiO2 composites when the lamp was continuously switched on and off first increased with increased Bio-CDs loading and then decreased; the transient photocurrent of TB9 was the highest and was significantly enhanced compared with that of pristine TiO2 (Fig. 7
a). The increased photocurrent is mainly caused by efficient separation and transfer of photogenerated electrons, which is beneficial for photocatalysis and correlates with photocatalytic performance [3,12,24,40–43]. EIS Nyquist plots are considered to be another valid method for studying the migration and interfacial transfer/recombination rates of charge carriers [3,12,24,42,43]. As shown in Fig. 7 b, as the loading of Bio-CDs increased, the radius of the circular arc gradually decreased and then increased. The radius of the arc was smallest for TB9, which means that the electrical impedance of TB9 is the smallest and that the charge transfer rate to the interface is the fastest [3,12,42,44]. This phenomenon should be attributed to the load of Bio-CDs promoting the electron transfer of TiO2 to Bio-CDs. The radius of the arc of electrical impedance in the presence of light is significantly smaller than that in the absence of light ( Fig. S3 ), further demonstrating that the speed of charge transfer to the interface is enhanced under visible light, which is consistent with the transient photocurrent responses. It is also worth noting that the LSV curves (Fig. 7 c) under illumination show that under the same current density, with the increase of the loading of Bio-CDs, the initial overpotential of Bio-CDs/TiO2 composites gradually decreases, indicating a gradual enhancement of proton reduction ability [3]. TB9 possesses the smallest initial overpotential, which is more conducive to proton reduction. The Tafel slope reflects the difficulty of hydrogen precipitation on the electrode. The smaller the slope, the faster the current density increases, and the easier for hydrogen to precipitate [45]. From the Tafel slope (Fig. 7 d) converted in Fig. 7 c, it can be seen that the photocatalytic hydrogen evolution efficiency of Bio-CDs/TiO2 composites increases with the increase of Bio-CDs loading. The Tafel slope value of TB9 is the smallest, indicating that TB9 has potential in photoelectrocatalytic hydrogen evolution. PL spectroscopy also provides important information for evaluating the separation of photogenerated carriers. As shown in Fig. 7 e, the PL intensity of TB9 is significantly lower than that of pristine TiO2 and the other composite samples, indicating that the recombination of photogenerated electron-hole pairs is significantly reduced in TB9 [3,12,30,32,40–42,44], in agreement with the above results. Time-resolved PL measurements were used to explore charge transfer events in the Bio-CDs/TiO2 composites. Notably, time-resolved PL studies have been used to characterize charge carrier dynamics since PL is the dominant pathway for recombination and dissipation of electron-hole pairs [3,40]. The time-resolved PL spectra of pristine TiO2 and TB9 were both fitted to a biexponential function (Fig. 7 f) and the two decay components of TiO2 were shortened upon coupling to the Bio-CDs ( Table S2 ). This phenomenon suggests that the Bio-CDs compete with the excited state recombination of TiO2 by providing an additional non-radiative pathway for charge carrier transfer, leading to a shortened PL lifetime and ubiquitous charge separation [39,46,47]. This enhances photocatalytic reduction and is consistent with the results of the photocatalytic hydrogen production experiments. The transient photocurrent responses, EIS Nyquist plots, LSV curves, PL and time-resolved PL all indicate that loading TiO2 with Bio-CDs promotes higher photogenerated carrier separation and faster charge transfer, thereby enhancing photocatalytic hydrogen production.

Based on Fig. 8 a, our proposed mechanism by which Bio-CDs promote photocatalytic hydrogen production by Bio-CDs/TiO2 is as follows. Under visible light, the Bio-CDs act as electron storage pools in the Bio-CDs/TiO2, enhancing the optical absorption of the Bio-CDs/TiO2 and promoting the transfer and storage of photogenerated electrons from TiO2 to the Bio-CDs. This delays the recombination of photoelectron-hole pairs, increases the separation of photogenerated carriers and enables more photogenerated electrons to participate in photocatalytic hydrogen production, thereby improving the photocatalytic reduction activity. This process was confirmed by TEM, transient photocurrent measurements, PL, EIS and FDTD simulations, and is considered to be the most plausible explanation for the enhanced catalytic hydrogen production under visible light. These results fully demonstrate that doping TiO2 with Bio-CDs delays recombination of electron-hole pairs under visible light through the synergistic effects of multiple mechanisms (enhanced optical absorption, increased separation of photogenerated carriers and promotion of transfer and storage of photogenerated electrons), thereby improving the separation efficiency of the photogenerated carriers and thus significantly improving photocatalytic hydrogen production and ensuring stable performance of the composite catalyst over multiple catalytic cycles.

In summary, Bio-CDs and TiO2 were bonded to form a composite material, Bio-CDs/TiO2, in which electron storage pools are created through Ti-O-C bonding, thereby improving the photocatalytic hydrogen production and stability of the TiO2-based photocatalysts. Structurally, the Bio-CDs nanoparticles are loaded on the surface of TiO2. Under visible light, the Bio-CDs enhance optical absorption and separation of the photogenerated carriers of the TiO2 and promote transfer and storage of electrons from TiO2 to Bio-CDs, thus effectively inhibiting the recombination of electron-hole pairs. The as-prepared Bio-CDs/TiO2 are excellent photocatalysts for hydrogen production. Over a 5-hour period, TB9 had the highest catalytic activity, with a hydrogen production rate of 603.92 μmol h−1 g−1, which is 6.41-fold higher than that of pristine TiO2. Importantly, the photocatalytic activity of TB9 was essentially unchanged after four cycles of hydrogen production, indicating its superior stability, which was confirmed by the Ti 2p XPS spectra before and after catalysis. In addition, under the combined action of photoelectricity, the loading of Bio-CDs significantly improves the photocatalytic hydrogen production ability of TiO2, with TB9 having the smallest Tafel slope for photocatalytic hydrogen evolution. The mechanism of formation of the Bio-CDs/TiO2 and the photocatalytic process were fully investigated by photoelectrochemical experiments and FDTD simulations. This study provides a good practical example of how to study the synergistic effects of the multiple mechanisms (enhanced optical absorption, increased separation of photogenerated carriers and promotion of transfer and storage of photogenerated electrons) by which carbon dots improve the photocatalytic hydrogen production of TiO2-based semiconductors.

Xiaolin Huang: Investigation, Methodology, Data curation, Formal analysis, Visualization, Writing – original draft. Lianhang Sun: Methodology, Data curation, Formal analysis, Validation. Xing Liu: Methodology, Data curation, Formal analysis, Validation. Min Ge: Methodology, Data curation, Formal analysis, Validation. Bin Zhao: Formal analysis, Visualization. Yibing Bai: Formal analysis, Visualization. Yuning Wang: Formal analysis, Visualization. Shiyan Han: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition. Yudong Li: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition. Youqi Han: Methodology, Formal analysis. Chunlei Zhang: Conceptualization, Supervision, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (32171715), the Natural Science Foundation of Heilongjiang Province (LH2022C012) and the Heilongjiang Postdoctoral Foundation (LBH-Q21003). The authors are grateful for the funding.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.apsusc.2023.157494.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Clean and affordable hydrogen fuel from alkaline water splitting: past, recent progress, and future prospects

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Biorefinery-assisted ultra-high hydrogen evolution via metal-free black phosphorus sensitized carbon nitride photocatalysis
